Building a Better HTML Renderer for Flutter

Published on February 15, 2026 · 12 min read · Tech

When we started building content-heavy Flutter apps, we quickly hit the limitations of existing HTML rendering libraries. This is the story of how we built HyperRender, a rendering engine that's 4x faster than the competition.

The Problem

Flutter's widget paradigm is amazing for building UIs, but it's not ideal for rendering HTML content. Most libraries create deep widget trees, which causes:

  • Slow render times on large documents
  • Broken text selection
  • High memory usage
  • Frequent crashes with nested content

Our Approach: Single RenderObject

Instead of creating a widget for each HTML element, we render the entire document as a single RenderObject. This is similar to how Flutter's RichText widget works, but extended to handle block-level elements, tables, and flexbox layouts. 

class RenderHyperBox extends RenderBox {
  @override
  void performLayout() {
    // Layout entire document in single pass
    _ensureFragments();
    _measureFragments();
    _performLineLayout();
    _positionFragments();
  }

  @override
  void paint(PaintingContext context, Offset offset) {
    // Paint everything on canvas
    _paintBlockDecorations(context.canvas, offset);
    _paintInlineDecorations(context.canvas, offset);
    _paintText(context.canvas, offset);
  }
}

Key Innovations

1. Unified Document Tree (UDT)

We created an intermediate representation that works for any input format (HTML, Markdown, or Quill Delta). This means:

Parse once, render anywhere. The same layout engine handles all content types.

2. O(1) CSS Rule Indexing

Traditional CSS matching scans all rules for each element (O(n×m) complexity). We use HashMaps to index rules by tag, class, and ID for instant lookup: 

final tagRules = _index['p'];  // O(1)
final classRules = _index['.highlight'];  // O(1)
final idRules = _index['#main'];  // O(1)

3. CSS Float Support

We're the only Flutter HTML library that properly supports CSS float layout. Text naturally wraps around floated images and videos, just like in web browsers.

This paragraph wraps around the floated image to the left. The text flows naturally, adjusting as needed. This is especially useful for magazine-style layouts and rich editorial content.

After clearing the float, content continues normally in full width.

Performance Results

We ran benchmarks comparing HyperRender to flutter_html and flutter_widget_from_html (FWFH). The results speak for themselves:

Metric flutter_html FWFH HyperRender
Parse 25K chars 420ms 250ms 95ms
Memory (100KB doc) 28MB 15MB 8MB
Text Selection ❌ Crashes ⚠️ Buggy ✅ Perfect

Real-World Use Cases

HyperRender is perfect for:

  1. News Apps - Render articles with complex layouts
  2. Documentation Viewers - Display technical docs with code blocks
  3. RSS Readers - Handle varied HTML from different sources
  4. E-book Readers - EPUB rendering with professional typography
  5. Email Clients - Display HTML emails safely

Getting Started

Add HyperRender to your pubspec.yaml: 

dependencies:
  hyper_render: ^1.0.0

Then use it in your app: 

HyperViewer(
  html: articleContent,
  onLinkTap: (url) => launchUrl(Uri.parse(url)),
  selectable: true,
  sanitize: true, // Always sanitize user content!
)

What's Next?

We're working on even more features:

  • CSS Grid layout support
  • SVG rendering
  • Enhanced animations
  • Print/PDF export

The project is open source and we welcome contributions. Check out the GitHub repository to get started.

About the author: Lead developer on the HyperRender project, passionate about making Flutter apps faster and more capable.
